content.claim: Hungary exhibits the highest or near-highest educational segregation in the EU: the socioeconomic achievement gap (53 points between graduate and non-graduate parent children) is 20 points above the EU average (33 points), and Hungary had the highest segregation of most and least disadvantaged students in the 2022 PISA study.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Portuguese system reform lens
limitations: Segregation is a system-wide phenomenon not attributable to gimnázium tracks alone
Roma/ethnic segregation (62% of Roma children in mono-ethnic schools per FRA 2025) is partly distinct from socioeconomic tracking effects
EU comparative data do not isolate causal contribution of early selection tracks to overall segregation
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
